title: Lesson 384
transcript: Okay, so what we're gonna do now is we're gonna start to go through some of the various components within a computer system. Now I wanna preface this. You absolutely do not need to be an expert on these components on things like the cpu the central processing unit or ram random access memory or hard drives or north bridges or south bridges or gpus or any of this stuff you absolutely do not need to be an expert on any of this specific hardware remember right this is a management level certification But I'm still gonna cover this stuff, why? Well, because you might get questions that will reference these things. The example I always love to use is, you might get a question in, say, digital forensics, as to why primary storage is particularly challenging from a digital forensics perspective. So the question is not specifically about primary storage. The question is about digital forensics, but you need to know what is primary storage to be able to answer that question. And we'll talk about, by the way, we'll talk about what primary storage is in just a minute here in the next video. So the point is. you need to know at a high level what some of these components are within your computer system and what they do. Not because you'll get direct questions on the components themselves, but you might get questions that reference these things, that reference a CPU or reference RAM. So you need to know at a high level, like what do these things do within the computer system? and of course i'm going to sprinkle in some security aspects of this as well that will be important to remember okay so let's start here let's start with processors cpus central processing units put overly simply cpus are essentially the brain of your computer system what cpus are doing incredibly quickly, blindingly quickly, is they're fetching instructions, decoding those instructions, executing them, and then storing the result. And they're doing this Billions of millions, maybe billions of times per second. Modern day CPUs are incredibly fast. And of course, what are they fetching? Code. They're decoding it, executing it, and then storing the results. And so this is why you can essentially think of the CPU as the brain of your computer system. The CPU is what's running the code, which is, of course, what makes the programs, what makes your computer system run. And I'll just highlight one more time, CPUs are doing this blindingly quickly. It'll be important to understand why in a second. Okay, now let's talk about CPUs from a security perspective. We're going to talk about what are known as the different processor states. What this is about is that in CPUs, there's essentially two different privilege levels. A higher privilege level where Any code that's running in that or any process that's running in that higher privilege level would essentially have access to all the CPUs capabilities. And then we have a lower privilege level where any process running in that lower privilege level would only have access to a limited set, not all the CPUs capabilities, just the more commonly used ones. So we have these two different privilege levels, supervisory state and problem state. And if you just had to guess based on the names, which do you think is the higher privilege level, supervisory state or problem state? Yeah, obviously supervisory state. And that's exactly right. Supervisory state is the higher privilege level. So any process that was running in supervisory state would have access to all the CPUs capabilities. And modern day CPUs can do all sorts of stuff. You wouldn't want... just any program or any process running on the system to be able to access. So that's why we have this higher privilege level that's reserved. The lower privilege level is known as problem state. Now you might be wondering, why is it called problem state? Like is the CPU having some sort of issue? No. I mean, if you think about what's the job of CPUs, CPUs are meant to solve problems. So problem state is actually the normal operating mode of the CPU. And the vast majority of processes running on your computer system, so like right now, whatever web browser you're watching this video in, Maybe you have Zoom open or Outlook or PowerPoint or whatever. Any of these processes would typically be running in problem state. So problem state is the lower privileges level that most processes, most applications will operate in. It's only a few very select processes that get to run in supervisory state. We'll get to that. OK. Now, one of the really cool things that our modern day computer systems can do is multitasking. We can have multiple processes running simultaneously, which is obviously super helpful from a productivity perspective. but it does introduce some security challenges. If we've got multiple processes running simultaneously, how do we make sure that we keep those processes separated so they can't negatively impact each other? like let's say process a here has a memory leak and eventually you know or there's some bug in the code and so process a crashes ideally right if one process on your system crashes you don't want that taking down the entire system so this is one reason that we want to keep processes separated so if one of them is having an issue it doesn't take out everyone else with it or maybe process A here ends up being a nefarious baddie process. You wouldn't want this malware, this process here to be able to get into and steal data from a bunch of other processes and things like that. simple point here is from a security perspective, it's really important to enforce process isolation, to keep these different processes separated, isolated from each other so they can't negatively impact each other. How do we achieve this? There's two major ways that we can achieve process isolation that you need to remember. Memory segmentation and time division multiplexing. So remember that. The two major ways of achieving process isolation are memory segmentation and time division multiplexing. So let's go through these and explain them. Here's what time division multiplexing is. Imagine there's a few processes running on your computer and those processes all want to be able to send and receive data. So let's say you're on a conference call on Zoom and you've also got your email program Outlook open. These two different processes, Zoom and Outlook, are both going to want to be able to send and receive data across the network. So they're both going to want to have access to the NIC, the Network Interface Card. Now, what we would not want is both Zoom and Outlook accessing the NIC at exactly the same time and conflicting with each other. So one way that we can keep these processes isolated is by implementing what's called time division, multiplexing. And the basic idea here is let's say we're talking about access to the NIC, the network interface card. And let's say we're looking at time going forward here. What we would do is we would slice up, we would slice the time into little slices here. Time division. So we're dividing up the time. And let's say each little bit of time here is, I don't know, one millisecond or something like that. That's not truly what it would be, but just so you understand this high level. We would divide up access to the network interface card into little one millisecond slices. And the whole idea here is that During one of these slices of time, it's going to be, say, Zoom's turn. So during this little slice of time here, Zoom will get complete access to the network interface card to send and receive whatever data it wants. And very importantly, when it's Zoom turn, nothing else has access to the network interface card. Outlook doesn't have access, whatever processes that might also want to be accessed to the internet or the network. None of them have access. It's just Zoom's turn. But once Zoom's turn is up, then it'll become maybe Outlook's turn. And so when it's Outlook's turn, Outlook can send and receive whatever data it wants, and none of the other processes have access to the NIC. And then, of course, you just keep switching back and forth, time, division, multiplexing. What you're doing is you're just switching back and forth between different processes. So maybe we go back to Zoom, and then we give it Outlook, and then maybe we switch to WebEx or whatever. So you're just switching back and forth, or your computer system is just switching back and forth. And of course, it's doing this so quickly that it looks like all the different processes all have access to the network interface card at the same time. But they don't. Each process only has access at one time. And this is a way, of course, of achieving process isolation, because no two processes are accessing the network interface card at the same time. So this is time division multiplexing, one way of achieving process isolation. Let's now talk about the other way of achieving process isolation, which is, as you can hear here, memory segmentation. Let's first start with what is memory? Random access memory. So as I said, and kind of highlighted a couple of minutes ago, modern day CPUs are blindingly fast, right? They can be performing billions of calculations per second. And of course, what's the CPU constantly doing? Fetching instructions, decoding execute, and storing the results. So the CPU constantly needs to be fetching data and storing data. Now, the major place that we store data on the computer system is on the hard drive. And even modern day, much faster, solid state hard drives, they're way faster than older hard drives. But compared to the CPU, even modern SSD drives are still glacially slow compared to the CPU. And this creates a big problem because the CPU had to constantly, every time the CPU needed some more data, if the CPU had to send that request directly to the hard drive and then wait for the hard drive to respond, it would slow your system to an absolute crawl. Because the CPU would say, OK, I need this data. And it would send that request to the hard drive. And the hard drive would glacially slowly go to look for the data, glacially slowly retrieve the data, glacially slowly send it back to the CPU. And the whole time the CPU would be sitting there going like, come on, come on, come on, come on. I'm waiting, I'm waiting, I'm waiting. Let's go. So it would really slow your system down if the CPU had to talk directly to the hard drive. So to massively speed up our systems, we have some intermediary storage space that we call RAM, Random Access Memory. RAM is much faster than the hard drive. And so the whole idea here, what happens is every time that you load up a new program, what's actually happening is instruction code and data is being copied off of the relatively slow hard drive, and it's being copied into the relatively much faster RAM. And then once all that instruction code and data has been loaded into RAM, the program will start running. And now if the CPU needs access to some data, to some instruction code, the CPU doesn't have to ask the really slow hard drive. The CPU can ask the much faster RAM because that data has been temporarily stored in RAM. So that's kind of the whole idea of RAM. It's this like temporary storage location for data and instruction code for any running processes. And here's where the process isolation thing comes in. Any process that's running in your computer system will have some instruction code and data loaded into RAM. So if you have Zoom running, there's going to be a little bit of memory space in RAM that's being allocated to Zoom. And if you have Outlook running, there's going to be a little bit of memory space allocated to Outlook. And of course, you have an operating system running. There's going to be a bunch of memory allocated to the operating system. And now here's where the process isolation part comes in. Obviously, if Outlook is accessing RAM, what should Outlook be able to access? Only its own bit of memory space and not another process's memory space or the operating system's memory space. So the whole idea here is Any program that's running on your computer system and any process that's running on your computer system is going to have some space allocated for it in RAM. And each process should only have access to its own memory space and not another process's memory space. That's memory segmentation, and it's a way of achieving process isolation. So one final time, right? The two major ways that we can achieve process isolation, memory segmentation and time division multiplexing. OK. Let's now talk about secure memory management, which is going to sound deeply familiar to one of the things that we just talked about. So let's say we have process A running here. And you can see here is memory. Here's our RAM. You can see we've got some memory space allocated to the operating system, some memory space allocated to process A, process B, process C. Which memory space should process A here be allowed to access? obviously its own memory space. Should it be allowed to access another programs, another processes memory space? No, definitely not. So of course, this sounds just like memory segmentation that we just talked about. So what's the difference between secure memory management and memory segmentation? The answer is this. Here's the subtle difference. Memory segmentation is what I just described there, right? It's where the operating system controls and makes sure that each process only has access to its own memory space. Secure memory management is actually a broader term that encompasses memory segmentation. And it also covers a few other ways that we can protect memory, basically, by trying to prevent things like buffer overflows and stuff like that. So just here's the subtle difference. Honestly, for the CISSP exam, they're basically the same thing. But the subtle differences is that memory segmentation is specific to making sure that a process can only access its own bit of memory space. And memory segmentation is one of the techniques contained within overall secure memory management. Overall secure memory management is, as the definition says here, focused on protecting memory from unauthorized access and corruption. And one of the ways that we can do that is through memory segmentation. It's also by doing things like bounds checking and so forth to try to prevent buffer overflow, stuff like that. So that's secure memory management, a broader term covering a bunch of different ways that we can try and protect memory.
